1: Melek (king, disp 2) is flagged twice -- King Hezekiah and Sennacherib king of Assyria. The historical narrative begins with a precise date (fourteenth year of Hezekiah = 701 BC) and two kings placed in deliberate contrast. Chapter 36 is nearly verbatim from 2 Kings 18:13-37 -- the narrative appears in both books, with minor variations. The MKT follows the Isaiah version. The great king title that Rabshakeh uses for Sennacherib (v4) contrasts with the LORD as the true king (Isaiah 33:22).
2: Melek is flagged twice -- the king of Assyria sending Rabshakeh and King Hezekiah receiving the challenge. Rabshakeh (the field commander / title, not personal name) arrives at the conduit of the Upper Pool on the fuller's field road -- the exact location where Isaiah had earlier confronted Ahaz (Isaiah 7:3). The geography is significant: the crisis point of the Assyrian confrontation is the same location as the earlier Syro-Ephraimite crisis, creating a structural echo across Isaiah's career.
3: Eliakim son of Hilkiah -- the steward installed in Shebna's place (Isaiah 22:20-22) -- comes out to meet Rabshakeh alongside Shebna (now the secretary, having been demoted) and Joah. The dramatic irony is visible: Shebna, against whom the woe-oracle was pronounced in Isaiah 22, is still present but reduced to a secondary role. The cast of characters makes explicit the connection between the oracle against Shebna and the historical crisis now unfolding.
4: Melek is flagged twice -- the Assyrian self-designation the great king, the king of Assyria is Sennacherib's formal imperial title, contrasted with Hezekiah addressed simply as Hezekiah without royal title. The rhetorical stripping of Hezekiah's royal title while inflating Sennacherib's is deliberate diplomacy-as-domination. The question -- what confidence is this in which you trust? -- frames the entire speech as an interrogation of Judah's source of security.
5: The Rabshakeh's argument: words without military strategy or battle strength are empty. The translation issue: the Assyrian is mocking the Judeans' verbal confidence (perhaps their recitation of the LORD's promises) as inadequate substitute for actual military capacity. The MKT's mere words are not strategy or battle strength captures the reductio ad militarem -- the Assyrian worldview that reduces all confidence to military calculation.
6: Melek is flagged -- the king of Assyria in the Rabshakeh's mouth. Egypt as a cracked and broken staff of reed -- if you lean on it, it pierces your hand. The Egypt-as-useless-ally taunt is historically accurate: Egypt had proved unable or unwilling to help Israel and Judah against Assyria (cf. Isaiah 30:1-5). The MKT's cracked and broken reed is vivid for the Hebrew kaneh haratsuts: the split/cracked stick of a reed.
7: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- the Rabshakeh's twisted argument: even if Judah trusts the LORD their God, hasn't Hezekiah just removed his high places and altars? The Assyrian misunderstands Hezekiah's reform (2 Kings 18:4) as impiety against the LORD, when in fact removing the high places was covenantal fidelity. The argument exposes how foreign the Assyrian framework is: he cannot distinguish between multiplying worship sites and faithful centralized worship.
8: Melek is flagged -- my master the king of Assyria. The wager of two thousand horses -- even if Sennacherib gives Judah cavalry, they cannot find enough riders. The taunt reveals the military asymmetry: Judah is not even capable of fielding the horses that Assyria could give them. The MKT's make a wager is a good idiomatic rendering of the Hebrew hitarev-na: engage/bet with my master.
9: The rhetorical escalation: not even one of the least of Sennacherib's junior officers could be resisted by Judah with Egyptian cavalry. The argument moves from the impossible (Judah can't use horses, v8) to the absurd (yet Judah relies on Egypt for chariots and cavalry). The MKT's junior officers is accurate for the Hebrew pechot: governors/deputies, the lowest level of Assyrian command.
10: YHWH is flagged twice -- the Rabshakeh's most audacious claim: The LORD himself said to me: Go up against this land and destroy it. Whether the Rabshakeh genuinely believed this (based on the Isaianic oracles about Assyria as the LORD's instrument, Isaiah 10) or was purely bluffing, the claim uses YHWH's own name to authorize the invasion. It is both theologically perceptive (Assyria was the LORD's instrument) and spiritually presumptuous (assuming that instrument-status meant permission for this specific act).
11: Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah ask Rabshakeh to switch to Aramaic (the diplomatic lingua franca) rather than Hebrew, which the general population on the wall can understand. The request for Aramaic is a containment strategy: keep the demoralizing speech from the ears of the people who might panic or defect. The three officials demonstrate awareness of the psychological warfare dimension of Rabshakeh's speech.
12: Rabshakeh refuses and escalates: the speech is precisely for the people on the wall, to whom it is now addressed directly. The graphic description of famine conditions -- eating their own dung and drinking their own urine -- is the Rabshakeh's depiction of what a siege will bring. The graphic content is an attempt to break the people's will before the siege begins. The MKT preserves the graphic language as the text requires.
13: Melek is flagged twice -- the Rabshakeh stands up and calls in Hebrew at full volume to the people on the wall. The public proclamation using the covenant people's own language is a deliberate move to bypass the official channels and appeal directly to the population. Hear the words of the great king, the king of Assyria -- the imperial title repeated as rhetorical thunder.
14: Melek is flagged -- this is the king's word. The Rabshakeh attributes the speech to Sennacherib himself: do not let Hezekiah mislead you -- he is not able to deliver you. The word deliver (natsil) is the key word of the chapter: Rabshakeh is disputing Hezekiah's claim that the LORD will deliver, by substituting the Assyrian king as the only effective agency. The theological claim embedded in the political speech is the real confrontation.
15: YHWH is flagged twice -- do not let Hezekiah lead you to trust in the LORD by saying, The LORD will certainly deliver us -- this city will not be handed over to the king of Assyria. The Rabshakeh quotes what Hezekiah is presumably saying to the people -- the Isaianic promise-formula that the city is under the LORD's protection. The Rabshakeh frames this faith as a deception. Melek is flagged -- the contest is between Hezekiah's LORD and Sennacherib's claim. The king of Assyria is used as a title of overwhelming power that the LORD cannot resist.
16: Melek is flagged -- the Rabshakeh speaks on behalf of the king of Assyria. The offer of terms -- make terms with me and surrender, eat from your own vine and fig tree -- is a parody of the Solomonic peace-picture (1 Kings 4:25; Micah 4:4). The good land promised (a land of grain, new wine, bread, vineyards, olive oil, honey) echoes the Deuteronomic promised-land formula (Deuteronomy 8:8) -- Sennacherib is offering an alternative promised land in deportation. The MKT's make terms with me and surrender preserves the condition of surrender embedded in the offer.
17: The promised deportation land is described in terms deliberately echoing the Promised Land -- grain and new wine, bread and vineyards. The Rabshakeh is rhetorically reversing the Exodus narrative: instead of the LORD bringing Israel out of Egypt into the Promised Land, Sennacherib offers to bring them out of Jerusalem into a replacement promised land in Assyria. The MKT preserves the agricultural abundance vocabulary that makes the parallel to the Promised Land explicit.
18: Elohim, YHWH, and melek are flagged -- Has any god of the nations ever delivered his land from my power? What makes you think the LORD will deliver Jerusalem? The Rabshakeh's comparative-religion argument: the gods of the nations (Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim) could not deliver their cities from Assyria. The argument treats YHWH as one deity among others, subject to the same evaluation by military track record. The flaw in the argument is precisely its comparatist framework: the LORD is not one deity among comparable deities.
19: Elohim is flagged twice -- where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim? Did they deliver Samaria from my power? The historical examples are accurate: Hamath, Arpad, and Sepharvaim had all fallen to Assyria. Samaria (the northern kingdom) had fallen in 722 BC -- within living memory of the Judeans hearing Rabshakeh's speech. The inclusion of Samaria is the most painful point: Israel's fall was supposed to be a warning to Judah, and Rabshakeh turns it into a threat.
20: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- Which of all the gods of these lands has ever delivered his land from my power? What makes you think the LORD will deliver Jerusalem? The speech closes with the summary argument: universal Assyrian supremacy over all the gods of all the nations. The Rabshakeh's theological claim is that divine power is measurable by military outcome -- and by that measure, Sennacherib wins. The flaw is the measure, not the data. The LORD's answer will be not a battle but an angel (Isaiah 37:36).
21: Melek is flagged -- the people's silence: they did not answer the Rabshakeh a word, for the king (Hezekiah) had commanded, do not answer him. The silence is disciplined obedience, not defeat. Hezekiah's command not to engage with Rabshakeh's speech reflects wisdom: engaging the speech on its own terms would be to accept its framework. Silence withdraws the legitimacy that a response would confer. The MKT's remained silent and said nothing in reply preserves the completeness of the non-engagement.
22: The three officials -- Eliakim the administrator, Shebna the secretary, Joah the recorder -- return to Hezekiah with their clothes torn in the sign of mourning and grief. The torn clothes are the appropriate response to blasphemous speech against the LORD: the same response Caiaphas will make at Jesus' claim to be the Son of Man (Matthew 26:65). The officials' report to the king completes the chapter's narrative arc from Rabshakeh's challenge to the delegation's return -- and prepares for Hezekiah's response in Isaiah 37.
